title: TikTok users don't have as much agency over their FYPs as they think
gist: A Northwestern algorithm audit using 90 cloned bot accounts found TikTok's negative feedback does work temporarily—the 'not interested' button cuts unwanted content ~84% versus 48% for skipping—but the algorithm relapses toward prior content unless users repeatedly reinforce feedback, and the effective button is deliberately hidden.

insights.claim: TikTok's 'not interested' button reduced unwanted content by around 84 percent, versus just a 48 percent reduction from merely skipping videos.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Quantifies which user controls actually move the algorithm, informing platform-design and regulatory debates over user agency.
insights.quote: The "not interested" button proved most effective, reducing unwanted content by around 84 percent, compared to just a 48 percent reduction from merely skipping videos.

insights.claim: The most effective 'not interested' control appears to be deliberately hidden from TikTok users despite being the strongest lever for curating the feed.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Suggests platforms design friction into effective opt-outs, a potential target for engagement-related regulation.
insights.quote: the authors note that the "not interested" option seems to be deliberately hidden from users.

insights.claim: Negative feedback works only temporarily; TikTok's algorithm gradually relapses and reintroduces unwanted content unless the user consistently repeats the same feedback, and even brief re-engagement is enough to trigger relapse.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Reveals the engagement-optimizing bias that overrides stated preferences, central to how recommendation platforms sustain watch time.
insights.quote: the platform does react to your negative feedback, but then it also very much reacts to your express behavior.
insights.locator: It turns out that it works in the beginning

insights.claim: TikTok's algorithm relies heavily on implicit signals such as watch duration in addition to explicit signals like likes or follows, and generally predicts user interests remarkably well.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Implicit behavioral signals dominate the recommender, explaining why stated 'not interested' preferences get overwhelmed by revealed behavior.

insights.claim: Researchers built 90 emulated bot 'sock puppet' accounts on the live TikTok app, intercepted network traffic for metadata, and used LLMs validated against human responses to make engagement decisions, focusing on cooking, fitness, and sports-betting topics.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a replicable sock-puppet audit method for studying black-box recommenders where official APIs are inadequate.
insights.quote: We used emulated devices, where we are creating accounts and automatically interfering with the TikTok algorithm through code with the sock puppet accounts

insights.claim: Neither TikTok's official researcher API nor the EU's researcher data-access regime exposes individual timelines, so personalization can only be studied by researchers collecting their own per-user data.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Highlights a gap in platform-transparency regulation that limits independent oversight of personalization algorithms.
insights.quote: none of the user agency is covered there. You can see what content is available, but you cannot see individual timelines
insights.locator: We basically work from the assumption

insights.claim: Users can be taught to use platform controls better, but the way they interface with the platform is ultimately dictated by fundamental design decisions.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Locates responsibility for feed outcomes in platform design rather than user behavior, reinforcing the case for design-level regulation.
insights.quote: the way that you're interfacing with the platform is going to be dictated by the design decisions that are fundamental to the platform
